customer_information.name_of_department.answer: Office of Vital Records and Health Data Management

jobs_information.if_print_job_is_ongoing.answer:
This confidential print and mail job is ongoing and is expected to continue for the duration of the current vital records modernization initiative, which is projected to run from 2026-01-01 through at least 2028-12-31. 

Operationally, the job will run on a recurring monthly cycle:
- Data file transmission to IDOA Print/Mail: between the 3rd and 5th business day of each month.
- Print window: 2–3 business days following data file receipt.
- Mail drop to USPS: no later than the 10th calendar day of each month.

In addition, there may be ad hoc supplemental runs up to once per quarter to address:
- Catch-up mailings for individuals whose records were corrected or updated after the primary monthly file was generated.
- Special communications related to policy or statutory changes affecting vital records handling.

We anticipate an average monthly volume of 8,000–12,000 pieces, with seasonal peaks in Q1 due to year-end reporting and insurance-related requests. The program will be reviewed annually at the end of each state fiscal year (June 30) to confirm continuation, modify volumes or business rules, or sunset the job if the project objectives have been met or system workflows have changed.

jobs_information.comments_additional_det.answer:
This job supports the Indiana Department of Health, Office of Vital Records and Health Data Management, in issuing confidential correspondence related to birth certificate amendments, adoption-related record updates, and corrections to vital records that contain personally identifiable information (PII) and, in some cases, partial Social Security numbers.

Scope and content of the job:
- Each mail piece is a personalized, 2–4 page letter explaining the nature of the vital record update, any required actions from the recipient, and contact information for follow-up.
- The letters may include:
  - Full legal name of the individual whose record has been updated.
  - Date of birth, place of birth, and certificate number.
  - Partial SSN (last four digits only) when required to validate the record match for the recipient.
  - Mailing address and, in some instances, the name of a legal representative (e.g., adoptive parent, legal guardian, or attorney of record).
- Approximately 15–20% of the letters relate to adoption or paternity establishment cases and may involve highly sensitive family information, which is why the confidentiality and secure handling of these print and mail jobs are critical.

File format and transmission details:
- The source data file will be generated from the state vital records system (IN-Vitals) in CSV format, encrypted using AES-256 and transferred via the state-approved secure file transfer protocol (SFTP) to the designated IDOA Print/Mail inbound directory.
- File naming convention: IDOH_VR_CONF_{YYYYMMDD}_BATCH{nn}.csv (e.g., IDOH_VR_CONF_20260304_BATCH01.csv).
- Each file includes a header row and record count, with a separate control file (.ctl) indicating file size, record count, and hash value for verification.

Print production details:
- Stock: 8.5" x 11" white security bond paper, 24 lb, with micro-printing in the footer to discourage unauthorized copying.
- Printing: Black and white, duplex (two-sided) where possible to reduce page count and postage, with a standard 0.5" margin on all sides.
- Envelopes: #10 security-tinted window envelopes. The recipient’s name and address will appear in the window; no confidential information should appear in the address block beyond name and mailing address.
- Return address: Indiana Department of Health, Office of Vital Records, 2 N. Meridian St., Indianapolis, IN 46204.
- Postage: Standard First-Class Mail, presorted when volume and USPS regulations permit.

Confidentiality and handling instructions:
- All printed overages, spoilage, and misprints must be kept in a secure, access-controlled area during production.
- After completion of each run and verification of counts, all overprints, test pages, and misprints must be destroyed on-site using cross-cut shredding or an equivalent state-approved destruction method. The "destroy" option is selected above to emphasize that no extra copies should be retained or returned to the agency.
- No electronic copies of the composed letters should be stored on non-state systems beyond what is minimally required for production and audit logging, and such copies should be purged according to IDOA and contract requirements.

Quality control and reconciliation:
- A daily production report should be generated for each batch, listing:
  - Batch file name
  - Date/time of receipt and completion
  - Number of records received vs. number of pieces mailed
  - Number of spoilage pieces and confirmation of destruction
- Any mismatch between file counts and mailed pieces must be reported to the agency contact (Laura M. Henderson) within one business day, with a description of the issue and remediation steps.

Contact and escalation details:
- Primary contact for business and content questions:
  - Name: Laura M. Henderson, Vital Records Program Manager
  - Phone: (317) 555-4823
  - Email: laura.henderson@health.in.gov
- Secondary contact for data file and technical issues:
  - Name: Daniel K. Ortiz, Health Data Systems Analyst
  - Phone: (317) 555-4972
  - Email: daniel.ortiz@health.in.gov

Examples of typical recipient scenarios:
- A parent receives a letter confirming a legal name change for their minor child’s birth certificate, with instructions on how to request updated certified copies.
- An adult adoptee receives a notification that a non-identifying amendment has been made to their sealed adoption record, along with guidance on what this means for future record requests.
- A legal guardian receives a letter documenting the correction of a date-of-birth error that previously caused mismatches with insurance and Social Security records, including what documentation they might need to share with other agencies.

Please ensure that the outer envelope clearly states "Important information enclosed" without referencing vital records, adoption, or any other sensitive topic on the exterior, to protect recipient privacy if mail is inadvertently seen by others at the household or workplace.
